exemplar.title: An Investigation Into The Effect of Word Length on Short-Term Memory

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation Into The Effect of Word Length on Short-Term Memory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, wellâstated aim with practical relevance explained
Thorough description of the Working Memory Model components
Explicit link between theory (phonological loop) and investigation
Both null and research hypotheses are correctly operationalised
weaknesses: Aim statement truncated in one location
Missing detail on specific methodological replication from Baddeley et al. (1975)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in the 5â6 band across all three dimensions. The aim is clearly stated and its relevance explained; theory is accurately described and connected; variables are fully operationalised. However, recurring minor errors in terminology, formatting, and incomplete methodological detail indicate room for improvement. A marginal deduction to 5/6 reflects this high quality overall with only slight lapses in precision.

strengths: Research design choice is not only described but justified in terms of variability control
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are detailed with rationale
Controlled variables and materials are listed with reasons for each
weaknesses: Terminology conflates stratified and mixed designs
Pilot study findings are mentioned but not summarized
Controlled variable choices not explicitly linked to validity improvements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Exploration strands meet the top (3â4) band with explanatory depth on design choice, sampling rationale, and material control. Minor weaknesses in methodological clarity, appendix referencing, and linking controls to validity warrant full but not perfect marks, so 4/4 is justified.

strengths: Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied
Findings interpretation clearly links data to hypothesis
Appropriate choice of nonparametric test (Wilcoxon) shown
weaknesses: Misapplication of Wilcoxon for independent groups instead of MannâWhitney U
Graph lacks axis labels, units, error bars, and caption
Inconsistencies in reported means and pâvalue notation errors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis shows solid understanding but contains a fundamental testâchoice error, placing it in the 3â4 band. Graphical presentation also meets the middle band due to missing labels and formatting flaws. Only the interpretation strand reaches the top band. The overall balance of appropriate methods mixed with key presentation and methodological errors supports a 4/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to the Working Memory Model and prior research
Strengths and limitations are relevant, explained, and linked to validity/reliability
Modifications are explicitly tied to limitations and well justified
weaknesses: Discussion lacks alternative explanations and boundaryâcondition analysis
Modifications could further explore tradeâoffs between validity and reliability
Generalizability and testâretest reliability are not evaluated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands consistently reach the 5â6 band: findings are discussed with theory, strengths and limitations are well explained, and modifications are clearly justified. However, deeper critical analysisâalternative explanations, broader implications, ecological validityâremains underdeveloped. A score of 5/6 reflects strong performance with scope for deeper critical insight.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of mean recall is provided and addresses the hypothesis, but it lacks axes titles, units and error bars; part of the title is truncated.  These presentation flaws place the work in the middle band where the graph âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errors.â

The student states that the mean for short-first condition exceeded that for long-first, cites the Wilcoxon p-value of 0.0001, explicitly **rejects the null and accepts the research hypothesis**, and links the outcome to the effect of word length.  Interpretation is clear and directly connected to both data and hypothesis, satisfying the 5-6 band; minor numerical rounding issues justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

Both null and research hypotheses are given, the IV (word length: 1â2 vs 4â5 syllables) and DV (number of names correctly recalled after 10 s) are **clearly stated and operationalised**.  Timing and scoring units are unambiguous, fully satisfying the top-band requirements.

Opportunity sampling is described and its convenience explained; participant characteristics (age, schooling, English level) are detailed and the rationale for using Grade 6 students is given (minimising demand characteristics).  This explanation of the **choice** of participants meets the higher band of Exploration.

The Working Memory Model is **accurately described**, with functions of the central executive, phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad and episodic buffer outlined.  The student then **explicitly connects** the phonological loop and the Word-Length Effect to the present investigation, explaining how rehearsal speed underlies expected differences in recall.  The thorough description and clear link meet the 5-6 band.

Findings are **discussed** in relation to Baddeley et al. (1975) and the phonological loop component of the Working Memory Model, explaining why shorter words are rehearsed more efficiently.  The discussion extends beyond mere description, although depth could be greater, so a solid 5 is appropriate.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether middle-school students recall more 1â2 syllable than 4â5 syllable country names after a 10-second interval â and then **explains its practical relevance** (implications for studentsâ note-taking habits).  The explanation moves beyond a mere identification and explicitly links the outcome of the study to a real-world educational context, satisfying the descriptor for the top band.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **explained and clearly linked** to aspects of the investigation (English-level control, vocabulary restriction, slideshow stress).  Each point is directly relevant to validity or reliability, fulfilling the top-band descriptor; the analysis is competent though not exhaustively critical, warranting 5.

Controlled variables (slide timing, unfamiliar word list, equal English proficiency, standardised instructions) are listed **and reasons are provided** (reducing human error, familiarity effects).  Materials such as the slideshow and pilot study are justified, fitting the 3-4 descriptor.

For each stated limitation, the student proposes a **specific, justified modification** (intervening task to counter practice effects, older sample to offset limited vocabulary, longer display time to reduce anxiety).  The direct linkage and justification satisfy the 5-6 descriptor; the explanations are adequate but not highly detailed, so 5 marks are awarded.

The student not only describes the stratified-measures (actually mixed) design but **explains why** it was chosen (control of English-level variability and order effects, increased generalisability).  Counter-balancing is justified.  This explanatory depth aligns with the 3-4 band.

Descriptive (means, SD) and inferential statistics are included.  The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test is appropriate for related data **if** each participant produced two scores, but in this report the two conditions appear to be independent groups, suggesting a **choice-of-test error**.  Nevertheless calculations are shown and interpreted.  Because statistics are generally appropriate but contain a possible methodological error, the strand fits the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The studentâs spelling of âBaddleyâ instead of the correct âBaddeleyâ suggests a need for careful proofreading of key terminology.
The description of Baddeley et al. (1975) lacks clarity on specific methodology details such as rehearsal pace and word selection; expanding this will strengthen the theoretical replication link.
The aim statement on this page is truncated (âafter a 10 secondâ); ensure the full aim is presented clearly and concisely in one place.
The independent variable is described only as âamount of syllablesâ without specifying the two levels (1â2 vs 4â5); explicitly operationalize these in the IV definition.
The dependent variable should specify scoring rules (e.g., recall in correct order vs any order) to avoid ambiguity in how recall is measured.
Hypothesis notation is inconsistent (âH1â vs âH_0â); adopt uniform formatting (e.g., Hâ and Hâ) and clarify if this is a one-tailed prediction.
The description of the âstratified measures designâ conflates mixed and stratified methods; clarify the true design and justify each element.
Opportunity sampling introduces selection bias; discuss its limitations for generalizability and consider alternative sampling approaches.
Procedure references âAppendix 1â for standardised instructions though Appendix 1 is the word list; correct appendix numbering and cross-references.
The pilot study is mentioned without summary of its findings; include pilot results to justify timing and materials.
The method of categorising âEnglish levelâ is not described; specify criteria or assessments used to stratify participants.
Controlled variables are listed but not linked explicitly to validity improvements; explain how each control strengthens internal validity.
The data table lacks a caption stating sample size (n=16) and context for independent vs related samples; add this to improve interpretability.
The text states M=2.95 for long-first condition, but the table shows 2.93; correct this rounding discrepancy.
The bar chart addresses the hypothesis but lacks axis labels, units and error bars; add these elements for clarity and completeness.
The figure has no concise caption describing its content and relevance; include a descriptive title and reference to the hypothesis.
The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test is for related samples; this study uses independent groups, so Mann-Whitney U would be more appropriate.
The inequality symbols for p-value interpretation are reversed; ensure âp â¤ 0.05â denotes significance and âp > 0.05â nonsignificance.
The evaluation restates findings but lacks critical analysis of alternative explanations or boundary conditions; expand discussion of theoretical implications.
Conclusion omits discussion of ecological validity; consider how slideshow recall replicates real-world memory tasks.
Some proposed modifications lack depth in justifying how they address trade-offs between validity and reliability; elaborate links.
The strengths/limitations discussion does not evaluate generalizability or transferability; discuss how sample choice affects broader application.
The conclusion fails to restate the investigation aim and integrate data in summarizing findings; refine for coherence.
The evaluation does not comment on testâretest reliability; consider discussing consistency of the slideshow methodology.
APA formatting is inconsistent in the Works Cited; include missing DOIs and follow consistent style guidelines.
Web sources lack retrieval dates and standardized formatting; revise to meet academic referencing conventions.
